coredata.dc:title: Sustainable carbon nanodots synthesised from kitchen derived waste tea residue for highly selective fluorimetric recognition of free chlorine in acidic water: A waste utilization approach
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of the Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2018 Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V. All rights reserved.
coredata.prism:publisher: Taiwan Institute of Chemical Engineers. Published by Elsevier B.V.
coredata.dc:creator: Gunjal, Datta B.
Naik, Vaibhav M.
Waghmare, Ravindra D.
Patil, Chandrashekhar S.
Shejwal, Rajendra V.
Gore, Anil H.
Kolekar, Govind B.
coredata.dc:description: A world wide easily available, economical, and sustainable carbonaceous source based on kitchen derived waste tea residue (containing sugar and milk) was used for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs). Pyrolysis of waste tea residue in controlled condition was employed to synthesis water soluble carbon dots (WTR-CDs). The as-synthesised WTR-CDs exhibited emission at 415 nm, excitation dependent behavior, stability towards the pH and ionic strength. The emission intensity of WTR-CDs dwindled with an addition of hypochlorite ion (ClO−) and designed sensor achieved sensitive detection of free chlorine rapidly with higher selectivity over twenty-one (21) common interfering ion tested. WTR-CDs showed good response over a range of 0.02–2.0 µg mL−1 of ClO−. Furthermore, the Stern-Volmer equation was used to obtain calibration curve displayed a very good linear response for the analyte within the range 0.02–0.30 µg/mL with the lowest limit of detection (LOD) is 0.038 µg/mL. Moreover, potential applications of this developed method were explored in various water bodies by standard addition method which finds a new avenue for the quantification of free chlorine and water quality monitoring.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Waste tea residue
Sustainable
Carbon dots
Fluorimetric recognition
Free chlorine
Water analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image (Inset: Particle size distribution graph) and (b) HR-TEM image of synthesized WTR-CDs.
(a) XRD pattern and (b) FT-IR spectrum of WTR-CDs.
XPS spectra of WTR-CDs; (a) XPS wide survey, High resolution spectra of (b) C1s spectrum (c) N1s spectrum and (d) O1s spectrum.
(a) UV–vis absorption, Excitation and Emission spectra of synthesized WTR-CDs, (b) The emission behaviour of the WTR-CDs at different excitation wavelength.
(a) Effect of pH (2–11) and (b) effect of ionic strength on fluorescence intensity of WTR-CDs.

(a) Fluorescence quenching spectra of WTR-CDs in the presence of different concentration of ClO− ions (b) Polynomial curve of fluorescence quenching. (Inset: Calibration curve of quenching in the range of 0.02–0.30 µg/mL).
(a) UV–vis absorption spectra of WTR-CDs and (b) Fluorescence decay spectrum for the WTR-CDs.
Effect of different interfering cations and anions on selective determination of ClO− in water samples.
Analysis result of OCl− ions in water samples from different water sources by the standard addition method (n = 3).

row.entry: Amount of standard OCl− ions added (µg/mL)

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Comparison of the proposed method (WTR-CDs) for the analysis of ClO−with some previously reported methods by using carbon-based nanomaterials.

head.ce:title: Sustainable carbon nanodots synthesised from kitchen derived waste tea residue for highly selective fluorimetric recognition of free chlorine in acidic water: A waste utilization approach

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Fluorescence Spectroscopy Research Laboratory, Shivaji University, Kolhapur-416004 Maharashtra, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Rajarshi Chhatrapati Shahu College, Kolhapur, 416005 Maharashtra, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Lal Bahadur Shastri College, Satara, 415002 Maharashtra, India

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Rajarshi Chhatrapati Shahu College, Kolhapur, 416005 Maharashtra, India.
Corresponding author at: Department of Chemistry, Fluorescence Spectroscopy Research Laboratory, Shivaji University, Kolhapur 416004 Maharashtra, India.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A world wide easily available, economical, and sustainable carbonaceous source based on kitchen derived waste tea residue (containing sugar and milk) was used for the synthesis of carbon dots (CDs). Pyrolysis of waste tea residue in controlled condition was employed to synthesis water soluble carbon dots (WTR-CDs). The as-synthesised WTR-CDs exhibited emission at 415 nm, excitation dependent behavior, stability towards the pH and ionic strength. The emission intensity of WTR-CDs dwindled with an addition of hypochlorite ion (ClO−) and designed sensor achieved sensitive detection of free chlorine rapidly with higher selectivity over twenty-one (21) common interfering ion tested. WTR-CDs showed good response over a range of 0.02–2.0 µg mL−1 of ClO−. Furthermore, the Stern-Volmer equation was used to obtain calibration curve displayed a very good linear response for the analyte within the range 0.02–0.30 µg/mL with the lowest limit of detection (LOD) is 0.038 µg/mL. Moreover, potential applications of this developed method were explored in various water bodies by standard addition method which finds a new avenue for the quantification of free chlorine and water quality monitoring.

ce:section.ce:para: Free residual chlorine is generally the sum of strongly oxidizing dissolved Chlorine (Cl2), hypochlorous acid (HClO) and hypochlorite (ClO−) ion. It has been widely used as disinfectants, blanching anddecolorizer in treating water since the years. The concentration of free residual chlorine is significant and should be maintained meticulously. The low concentration of free residual chlorine is too negligible to kill pathogens and viruses in water [1]. However, a higher level may produce a large number of undesirable trihalomethanes as by products [2]. These compounds were known for their harmful effect to human beings and animals. It causes respiratory problems, burning sensations, skin and throat irritation, the harm of the cardiovascular system, abdominal pain, coughing and vomiting, cancer and reproductive failure[1]. Thus, ultimately free chlorine inevitably poses a safety hazard to human health. Therefore, it becomes extremely necessary to monitor and control the level of chlorine in water bodies. To date, many methods have been put forth by the researchers for the determination of free chlorine, including the chemiluminescence [3], ion chromatography [4], colorimetry [5], gas chromatography [6], liquid chromatography [7], Spectrophotometry [8], flow injection analysis [9] and so on. Although, these methods have its advantages, but also have some of their respective disadvantages such as requirements of severe synthetic conditions, tedious and time-consuming processes, high temperature, high cost, poor selectivity and the use of toxic reagents. Therefore, developing a facile, sensitive and effective method for the determination of free chlorine in water bodies is still of worthful.

Carbon nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, fullerenes, carbon dots (CDs), graphene quantum dots and nanofibers became a center of attraction in recent years owing to their promising applications in various fields such as sensing, bioimaging and drug delivery. However, among these nanomaterials, carbon dots have tempted world research community because of their massive applications and ease route of synthesis compared to the other family members. It was unintentionally discovered during electrophoretic purification of single-wall carbon nanotubes in 2004 [10]. It is generally quasi-spherical (size below 10 nm) in shape and composed with abundant hydrogenous and oxygenous residues with SP2 hybridized carbon atoms [11,12]. These CDs possesses some magnificent properties such as higher aqueous solubility, high specific surface area, low toxicity, higher resistance to photobleaching, better biocompatibility, low cost, and fluorescent properties. Moreover, their surface could be easily tuned to the specific functionalities of interest. All these properties of CDs are superior to the environmental hazardous fluorescent semiconductor quantum dots and traditional organic dyes. Eventually, these fascinating features make them promising alternative material to these compounds and created enormous excitement among the researcher [13]. Therefore, CDs was widely applied in the areas such as photocatalysis [14], sensing [15], optoelectronic devices [16], biosensors and bioimaging applications [17–20]. After the inception of CDs, different methodologies have been exercised for their synthesis. The synthetic routes such as hydrothermal [21], acidic oxidation [22], electrochemical oxidation [23], microwave [24], ultrasonic [25], laser ablation [26] and arc discharge [10] methods were explored to synthesize CDs. In most of the techniques, either fine carbon structures or conventional chemicals such as carbohydrates, citric acid, amino acids [13] and so on, were used as a precursor to the synthesis of the fluorescent CDs. But recently, renewable resources as starting materials have been used by many scientists and gained much success also. Numerous “green” raw materials such as pomelo peel, gas soot, orange juice, soybeans, watermelon peel, kitchen waste, grass etc [27] were employed to synthesis biocompatible CDs. Nevertheless, overwhelming availability of carbon rich waste material finds extensive space for research with this context. So, exploring alternative simple, cost effective, environmentally friendly, easy control synthetic route of CDs is still highly desirable.

Recently, Bangda Yin et.al, has synthesized CDs from pepper through one step hydrothermal method and successively applied for the quantification free chlorine in the water [28]. However, cost of production of CDs, limit of detection of this system is still high and pepper is a valuable kitchen material which further confronts their use and applicability of system. In this work, we focused on simple, cost-effective, one step carbonization approach for the synthesis of fluorescent CDs from kitchen waste derived tea powder, ubiquitously available source of biomass. The waste tea material not only contains tea powder but some valuable carbon rich source like sugar and milk protein also. All these waste materials cumulatively serve as sources of carbon for the preparation of CDs. We also found that direct pyrolysis is a much simpler procedure for obtaining CDs than other known techniques. The as synthesized WTR-CDs were further applied for the determination of free chlorine in different water system based on the fluorescence quenching. The established method was found to be a sensitive, highly selective, low-cost, rapid, facile and greener one.

The waste tea residue powder was made available from a Canteen, Shivaji University Kolhapur and cleaned by collecting unwanted solid materials. All analytical grade reagents required for Carmody buffer preparations were procured from Lobachemie (Mumbai, India). By dissolving appropriate quantity of trisodium orthophosphate (Na3PO4.12 H2O), boric acid (H3BO3) and citric acid (C6H8O7.H2O), buffers solutions of different pH were prepared [29]. Sodium hypochlorite solution was procured from s-d fine chemicals. Standard solution (100 µg/mL) of hypochlorite ion (OCl−) was prepared by diluting appropriate volume of 4% NaOCl liquid stock. All the other chemicals were of analytical grade and were used as received. Double distilled water was used throughout the experiment.

The as synthesized WTR-CDs were characterized by various analytical techniques. All the emission spectra were recorded in the range of 330–600 nm upon excitation at 315 nm on a FP-8300 fluorescence spectrometer (Jasco). The UV–vis absorption spectra were obtained by a Specord 210 plus UV–vis spectrophotometer (Analytica jena). The transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images were obtained on JEOL-1230 Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) (FEI, Tecnai G2, F30) by placing a drop of the WTR-CDs solution on the carbon-coated copper grid and dried at ambient temperature. The chemical composition was defined by Kratos AXIS ULTRA X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). While X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern was obtained on D/max-2500 V/PC powder X-ray diffractometer at an angle 2θ (Rigaku, Japan). The surface functionality of WTR-CDs was recognized by Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) Spectrometer (Perkin-Elmer, USA). The decay curve experiments were recorded on a TCSPC time-resolved fluorescence spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Jvon) employing a nanosecond diode laser (IBH, Nano LED-07).

The luminescent WTR-CDs were synthesized by a one-step carbonization method using kitchen waste tea residue (WTR) as a carbon source. Firstly, WTR powder (residue after the tea preparation) was dried as such received at room temperature aimed to keep another carbonaceous material into it. This was further ground into fine powder. Then, 2.0 g of obtained powder was transferred into silica crucible and heated at 200 °C for 5 h in a muffle furnace. The heated black powder was then allowed to cool at room temperature. Furthermore, the grounded black fine powder was dispersed into distilled water and stirred overnight. This resultant mixture was filtered through filter paper and an aliquot was then centrifuged at 12,000 rpm to remove larger particles. The supernatant was subjected for syringe filtration (0.22 µm) to obtain fine WTR-CDs. Then the whole solution was diluted to specific volume and kept at the free temperature for further experimental study. The concentration of resultant WTR-CDs solution is 6.55 mg/mL.

The quantum yield (QY) of the synthesized WTR-CDs was measured by the comparative method. The aqueous solution of quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (refractive index η of 1.33, QY = 0.54) was chosen as a standard compound. The integrated fluorescence intensities and the absorbance values of the WTR-CDs and reference were used to calculate the QY of WTR-CDs by using following equation.
(1)
Q Y = Q
Y R
×
I
I R
×
A R
A
×
η 2
η R 2

Where, QY is the quantum yield of WTR-CDs, I is the integrated fluorescence intensity, A represents the absorbance while η is the refractive index of the solvents. The subscript R refers to the standard compound.

The detection of free chlorine was carried out at ambient temperature. In a typical assay, the 0.5 mL of CDs dispersion (1.0 mg/mL) was mixed with 1.0 mL buffer solution (pH = 3) followed by the addition of different concentrations of standard hypochlorite solution and finally, the whole mixture was diluted to 10 mL with double distilled water. This solution was allowed to react for 5 min at room temperature and finally the fluorescence spectra were recorded at 315 nm excitation. The selectivity of the proposed fluorescent probe was observed by adding stock solutions of different anions instead of free chlorine in a similar way. The slit width for both the monochromator was fixed at 5 nm throughout the experiment.

Four different real samples such as tap, swimming pool and two types of mineral water samples were collected from respective sources. All these samples were employed for analysis with nominal pre-treatment (boiling and filtration) in order to remove suspended organic and clay particles. Aliquots of each water samples were spiked with three altered concentrations of standard hypochlorite solutions. Afterward, these samples were added to the developed sensing system, equilibrated for 5 min and then spectral measurement was recorded.

The as synthesised WTR-CDs were highly water soluble and characterised by different optical and surface analysis techniques. The particle size and morphology of WTR-CDs were analysed by TEM. Typical TEM images (Fig. 1 a) shows uniform distribution of almost spherically shaped particles in aqueous medium with an average size 5 nm. The high resolution TEM (HR-TEM) image displays 0.25 nm lattice spacing distance (d) which may be correlated with (100) fringes distance of graphite carbon, indicates synthesised WTR-CDs might have graphitic carbon (Fig. 1b) [30]. In addition to that XRD analysis of WTR-CDs indicates broader peak centred at 23.96° having (002) diffraction planes correspond to the 0.37 nm interlayer distance which is bigger than that of graphite (0.34 nm) (Fig. 2 a) [31]. In situ introduction of oxygen containing groups during the synthesis process might be responsible for increasing the interlayer distance. This results are in good agreement with TEM analysis and attributed to highly disordered and graphitic nature carbon atoms of WTR-CDs [28].

Surface functionality of CDs was recognised by FTIR spectroscopy. The absorption band at 3306/cm accounts for O-H or N-H stretching vibrations. The absorption band at 1626/cm attributes to the C=C stretching vibration indicates the presence of a sp2 hybridized carbon. Besides, stretching frequency at 1220/cm ascribed to the C-O/C-N groups and absorption at 1370/cm is responsible for -CH3 deformation/C-H bending. All of the above FT-IR absorption reveals that the presence of hydroxyl and carboxyl functional groups on the surface of the WTR-CDs. These hydrophilic groups impart excellent water solubility to the synthesised CDs [32]. Furthermore, the chemical composition and surface states of synthesised WTR-CDs was evaluated by the XPS technique. The XPS spectrum [Fig. 3 a] of WTR-CDs shows two strong elemental peaks of C1s, and O1s and weak peak of N1s at 286.35, 532.66 and 399.82 eV, respectively. The deconvoluted C1s spectrum [Fig. 3b] displays three peaks at 283.83, 285.36, and 287.29 eV attributed to the C=C, C-OH/C–N, and C=O groups, respectively [32]. The peak at 283.83 further supports the presences of sp2 carbon atom in synthesised material. Although nitrogen shows very weak elemental peak in wide spectra, their high-resolution spectra indicates the existence of nitrogen. Fig. 3c displays high-resolution N1s spectrum, shows two binding energy peaks at 398.97 eV and 401.05 eV which may be due to the C–N–C and N–H bands [33]. Moreover, in the XPS fitting of O1s spectrum (Fig. 3d),two representative peaks at 531.41 eV and 534.71 eV, could be associated with C=O and N-C=O bands[32]. Thus, XPS results strongly suggested presences of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen element and various functional groups in the prepared WTR-CDs which are consistent with FT-IR spectroscopy.

The optical properties of WTR-CDs were explored by UV–visible and fluorescence spectroscopy. The absorption spectrum of synthesized material (Fig. 4 a) showed three characteristic peaks at 259, 319 and 371 nm. The absorption peak at 259 nm could be associated with the π–π* transition of C=C core whereas peak at 319 and 371 corelates to the n–π* transitions of C=N or carbonyl (C=O) group of WTR-CDs [34]. The Fig. 4a further represents the fluorescence spectra of WTR-CDs. It indicates maximum emission wavelength lies at 415 nm to the corresponding 315 nm excitation wavelength; suggested synthesized WTR-CDs have bright fluorescence property. However, origin fluorescence of CDs is still under investigation, it is assumed that it may due to the surface trap states, surface defects etc. [35]. The quantum yield of prepared WTR-CDs was measured at their excitation wavelength according to the Eq. (1) and was found to be 4.76%. Thus, the quantum yield and fluorescence properties strongly suggest that the as synthesized WTR-CDs have durable fluorescence. Moreover, the fluorescence properties of WTR-CDs have been studied by changing the excitation wavelength from 280 to 520 nm. It was noticed that increasing excitation wavelength accompanied with a gradualredshift of the emission peaks with variable emission intensity [Fig. 4b], owing to the surface emissive trap states and different particle sizes of WTR-CDs. This reveals that synthesized WTR-CDs also shows excitation dependent emission wavelength which is similar to the previous reports [22].

The fluorescence stability of synthesized WTR-CDs was investigated in different pH solutions and in high ionic strength medium. Fig. 5 a reveals that the emission intensity of WTR-CDs varies randomly with pH of the solution. It shows maximum intensity in highly acidic condition (at pH 3), remained almost stable from pH 4 to 7 while progressively decreased from pH 8 upon excitation at 315 nm. The dependency of the fluorescence of WTR-CDs on pH might be ascribed to the acidic sites responsible for the emission since fluorescence is quenched in basic region[36]. The effect of ionic strength (Fig. 5b) on emission intensity of WTR-CDs was also studied in different concentrations of NaCl. Merely a small variation in fluorescence intensity was observed, suggested that WTR-CDs have considerable resistance ability to high ionic strength conditions.

The fluorescence response of WTR-CDs was analyzed with different concentrations of hypochlorite under the optimized experimental conditions. Fig. 6 a shows that the progressive decline of the fluorescence of WTR-CDs with increasing concentration of hypochlorite, an almost 75% of the emission signal was quenched by 2.0 µg/mL ClO−(at pH = 3.0) reveals that proposed system is sensitive to the analyte concentrations. Therefore, we speculated that quantification of free chlorine could be achieved easily with the developed probe.

In order to develop the method for the determination of any analyte, there should be a linear correlation between analyte concentration and signal intensity. Herein, we observed the non-linear relation between fluorescence signals of the probe to the whole experimental concentration range of ClO− (Fig. 6b). Hence to evaluate the analytical linear range, standard Stern-Volmer quenching relationship was employed.
(2)
F 0
F
= 1 +
K S
V
[ Q ]

Where F0and F is the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of ClO− ions, Ksv is the Stern–Volmer constant and [Q] is the concentration of ClO− ions. Inset of Fig. 6b is the plot of F0/F versus concentration of ClO− and demonstrates a linear relationship between fluorescence response and the concentration of ClO− ions. It was obtained within the range of 0.02–0.30 µg/mL with the correlation coefficient (R2) 0.999. Subsequently, the limit of detection (LOD) for ClO− ions was evaluated by using following equation
(3)
Limit
of
Detection =
3 σ
K

Where, σ is standard deviation and K is the slope of the calibration curve. By using Stern-Volmer plot, the LOD of the developed sensing system was found to be 0.038 µg/mL .Aforementioned linearity, correlation coefficient, and LOD demonstrate the satisfactory precision of this sensing system which makes it as a promising method and could be explored for assaying hypochlorite in water samples (Table 1 ).

The oxidizing ability of free chlorine is a premier cause of fluorescence quenching. Generally, free chlorine exists as HClO in the acidic and weakly alkaline medium while in ClO− form in strongly basic condition. Among these two forms, HClO has stronger oxidative nature than ClO− and eventually higher fluorescence quenching also [37]. As per observed in Fig. 5a the maximum quenching efficiency of ClO−is at pH 3. Therefore, we have adjusted acidic pH of the sensing system throughout the experiment (pH = 3). HClO oxidizes the surface hydroxyl and carboxyl group of WTR-CDs which leads to the structural changes in it and eventually quenching of fluorescence. This is confirmed by absorption spectrum. Fig. 7 a shows the change in characteristic absorption spectra of WTR-CDs with the addition of hypochlorite ion. All three absorption peaks of WTR-CDs at 259, 319 and 371 nm diminished and a new shoulder like absorption peak at 288 nm was observed which was attributed to the oxidation or complex formation between a surface functional group of probe and analyte. This is also further confirmed by lifetime measurement.

Fluorescence quenching is either static or dynamic type. In Static quenching formation of ground state complex and collision at excited state in dynamic type are main factors responsible for quenching of fluorescence. Fluorescence lifetime measurement widely used and reliable methods to distinguish between these two types of quenching. TCSPC experiment was performed at the excitation wavelength of WTR-CDs for 415 nm emission wavelength and biexpotential fit decay curve was obtained. Fig. 7b indicated the lifetime of WTR-CDs in absence and presence of different concentrations of ClO− ions and it was found to be 2.41, 2.35 and 2.37 ns. These results revealed that virtually no change in a lifetime and hence static quenching predominates in the performed experiment. Thus, the change in UV–vis spectrum and almost stagnant fluorescence lifetime of the system indicated free chlorine could disturb the surface groups of WTR-CDs, leading to significant changes in the structure of probe and eventually responsible for fluorescence quenching.

The selectivity of the proposed method is an important aspect in a practical point of view so investigated thoroughly with the same experimental condition (pH = 3). Different environmentally relevant anions and cations such as Cl−, S2O3 −, CH3COO−, HCO3 , BrO3 −, I−, IO3 −, SO4 2−, F−, SO3 −, NO3 −, PO4 3−, HPO4 2−, SCN−, S2−, CO3 2−, K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+and Na+(10 µg/mL each) were examined with developed probe. Fig. 8 shows a discernible change in fluorescence intensity with the addition of hypochlorite ion (0.05 µg/mL) owing to their oxidizing ability of the surface hydroxyl and carboxyl group of WTR-CDs which leads to the structural changes in it and eventually quenching of fluorescence while other ions did not show the noticeable effect on intensity although used in higher concentration than ClO− ion. More importantly easily reducible sulphur containing interfering ions had negligible change on the fluorescence of probe. All these results reflect that the fluorescence quenching of the CDs is selective to ClO−rather than common ions present in water. Thus, sensitivity, rapid response to the quenching and selectivity suggested that developed probe could be employed as an efficient fluorescent sensor in monitoring ClO− in water samples. The results of the proposed method also compared with previous reports (Table 2 ). Our developed method shows comparable linear range and limit of detection with the existing methods. We have synthesised fluorescent carbon dots by utilizing waste biomass which confers inexpensiveness and sustainability.

The feasibility of the proposed approach for detection of ClO−in real water samples was evaluated with different water samples and spiked with various concentrations of standard ClO−solution. As predicted, emission intensity decreases with increasing amount of hypochlorite ion. By using calibration curve, percent recoveries and relative standard deviation were calculated. As listed in Table 1, all recoveries were in between 95–104% while relative standard deviation within 0.58–1.15 indicating this method is highly accurate and reliable for determination of ClO− in real samples.

We adopted a simple carbonization route for synthesizing WTR-CDs from waste kitchen biomass which is low cost and abundantly available. So that our method become eco-friendly, cheap, greener one over the other methods. As synthesized WTR-CDs are highly stable in ionic medium, shows excitation dependent emission while the variable response to the pH. Furthermore, it has shown remarkable fluorescence quenching to ClO− ion owing to the oxidative nature of ion. This developed sensor has advantages like sensitivity and highest selectivity over twenty-one (21) common interfering ions tested, low detection limit compared to the other biomass derived CDs, and fast response which made a promising platform for detection of ClO−in real samples with good accuracy and credibility.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors AHG and DBG gratefully acknowledges DST-SERB and UGC, New Delhi for providing research grants under young scientist scheme (File no. YSS/2015/001806 ) and JRF [F.17-11/2008(SA-I)] fellowship respectively.
